name: GitHub Practice Exam
description: 77 scenario-based questions from the community CCA-F practice exam

questions.question: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your agent skips `get_customer` entirely and calls `lookup_order` using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. What change would most effectively address this reliability issue?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks `lookup_order` and `process_refund` calls until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID
options.explanation: Programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees. A PostToolUse hook or prerequisite gate physically blocks tools until verification completes. Zero% failure rate. CODE > PROMPTS.

options.label: B
options.text: Enhance the system prompt to state that customer verification via `get_customer` is mandatory before any order operations
options.explanation: Prompt instructions are probabilistic â€” the 12% skip rate exists despite the model generally knowing the right sequence. When errors have financial consequences, probabilistic compliance is unacceptable.

options.label: C
options.text: Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling `get_customer` first, even when customers volunteer order details
options.explanation: Few-shot examples improve consistency but are still prompt-based guidance. They add token overhead and remain probabilistic. Same fundamental limitation as B.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement a routing classifier that analyzes each request and enables only the subset of tools appropriate for that request type
options.explanation: A routing classifier addresses which tools are available, not what order they must be called in. Solves tool selection, not tool sequencing â€” which is the actual problem.

questions.algorithmTrace: B and C are prompt-based → anti-pattern for deterministic business rules → eliminate. D solves wrong problem. A is programmatic enforcement → CODE > PROMPTS → select A.

questions.question: Production logs show the agent frequently calls `get_customer` when users ask about orders (e.g., "check my order #12345"), instead of calling `lookup_order`. Both tools have minimal descriptions and accept similar identifier formats. What's the most effective first step to improve tool selection reliability?

options.label: A
options.text: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection patterns, with 5â€“8 examples
options.explanation: Few-shot examples add token overhead without fixing the root cause: the model can't distinguish between tools because descriptions are minimal. Descriptions are the primary selection signal.

options.label: B
options.text: Expand each tool's description to include input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries explaining when to use it vs similar tools
options.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection. Expanding them directly addresses the root cause. Low-effort, high-leverage fix. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a routing layer that parses user input and pre-selects the appropriate tool based on detected keywords
options.explanation: A keyword-based routing layer bypasses the LLM's natural language understanding and is fragile to edge cases. NL parsing for control flow anti-pattern.

options.label: D
options.text: Consolidate both tools into a single `lookup_entity` tool that accepts any identifier and internally determines which backend to query
options.explanation: Valid architectural choice but requires backend changes. The question asks for the most effective first step â€” description improvement is faster and often sufficient.

questions.algorithmTrace: C = NL parsing anti-pattern → eliminate. A adds overhead without fixing root cause. D is valid but heavy for a 'first step'. B is built-in mechanism → BUILT-IN > CUSTOM → select B.

questions.question: Your agent achieves 55% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Logs show it escalates straightforward cases while attempting complex situations requiring policy exceptions. What's the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?

options.label: A
options.text: Add explicit escalation criteria to your system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate vs resolve autonomously
options.explanation: The root cause is unclear decision boundaries. Adding explicit criteria with few-shot examples directly fixes the calibration. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1â€“10) and automatically route requests to humans below a threshold
options.explanation: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated â€” the agent is already incorrectly confident on hard cases. A confidence score encodes the same bad calibration numerically.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation
options.explanation: Over-engineered, requiring labeled data and ML infrastructure when prompt optimization hasn't been tried. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration levels and automatically escalate when threshold exceeded
options.explanation: Sentiment-based escalation is an explicit exam anti-pattern. Sentiment doesn't correlate with case complexity â€” an angry customer with a standard replacement is still simple.

questions.algorithmTrace: B = self-reported confidence → unreliable → eliminate. D = sentiment-based escalation → anti-pattern → eliminate. C = over-engineered. A = explicit criteria → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT → select A.

questions.question: You want to create a custom `/review` slash command that runs your team's standard code review checklist. This command should be available to every developer when they clone or pull the repository. Where should you create this command file?

options.label: A
options.text: In the `.claude/commands/` directory in the project repository
options.explanation: `.claude/commands/` is the project-scoped command directory. It's version-controlled and automatically available to all developers when they clone or pull the repo.

options.label: B
options.text: In `~/.claude/commands/` in each developer's home directory
options.explanation: `~/.claude/commands/` is for personal commands not shared via version control. Each developer would need to manually create the file.

options.label: C
options.text: In the `CLAUDE.md` file at the project root
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md is for project instructions and context, not command definitions.

options.label: D
options.text: In a `.claude/config.json` file with a `commands` array
options.explanation: `.claude/config.json` with a `commands` array is a fabricated configuration mechanism that does not exist in Claude Code. Trap answer.

questions.question: You've been assigned to restructure the team's monolithic application into microservices. This will involve changes across dozens of files and requires decisions about service boundaries. Which approach should you take?

options.label: A
options.text: Enter plan mode to explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design an implementation approach before making changes
options.explanation: Plan mode is designed for complex tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, and architectural decisions â€” exactly what monolith restructuring requires.

options.label: B
options.text: Start with direct execution and make changes incrementally, letting the implementation reveal natural service boundaries
options.explanation: Starting with direct execution risks discovering dependencies late, leading to costly rework. Service boundaries require understanding the entire system first.

options.label: C
options.text: Use direct execution with comprehensive upfront instructions detailing exactly how each service should be structured
options.explanation: Assumes you already know the right structure without exploring the code. For a monolith, the right boundaries are unknown until you explore dependencies.

options.label: D
options.text: Begin in direct execution mode and only switch to plan mode if you encounter unexpected complexity
options.explanation: The complexity is already stated in the requirements â€” it's not something that 'might emerge later.' Ignoring obvious complexity wastes time.

questions.question: Your codebase has test files spread throughout (e.g., `Button.test.tsx` next to `Button.tsx`). You want all tests to follow the same conventions regardless of location. What's the most maintainable way to ensure Claude automatically applies correct conventions?

options.label: A
options.text: Create rule files in `.claude/rules/` with YAML frontmatter specifying glob patterns to conditionally apply conventions based on file paths
options.explanation: `.claude/rules/` with glob patterns (e.g., `paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"]`) allows conventions to be automatically applied regardless of directory location. Deterministic loading, zero manual invocation.

options.label: B
options.text: Consolidate all conventions in the root CLAUDE.md file under headers for each area, relying on Claude to infer which applies
options.explanation: Relies on Claude inferring which section applies â€” unreliable. Also wastes tokens loading all conventions when only some are relevant. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: C
options.text: Create skills in `.claude/skills/` for each code type that include the relevant conventions
options.explanation: Skills require manual invocation, contradicting the need for deterministic 'automatic' application based on file paths.

options.label: D
options.text: Place a separate CLAUDE.md file in each subdirectory containing that area's specific conventions
options.explanation: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files are directory-bound. If test files are spread across many directories, you'd need duplicate files everywhere â€” unmaintainable.

questions.question: After running the system on "impact of AI on creative industries," the final reports cover only visual arts, missing music, writing, and film. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed into: "AI in digital art creation," "AI in graphic design," and "AI in photography." What is the most likely root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps
options.explanation: The synthesis agent can only synthesize what it receives. It received only visual arts findings because that's all subagents were assigned. Blames wrong agent.

options.label: B
options.text: The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in incomplete coverage
options.explanation: The coordinator's logs directly reveal the root cause: it decomposed 'creative industries' into only visual arts subtasks. Subagents executed correctly â€” the problem is what they were assigned.

options.label: C
options.text: The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough
options.explanation: The web search agent searched for what it was told to search for. Its queries were scoped by the coordinator's assignment. Can't blame the search for not searching unassigned topics.

options.label: D
options.text: The document analysis agent is filtering out non-visual creative industry sources
options.explanation: The document analysis agent was never given non-visual sources to analyze. It didn't filter anything â€” it simply worked within its assigned scope.

questions.question: The web search subagent times out while researching a complex topic. You need to design how this failure information flows back to the coordinator. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?

options.label: A
options.text: Return structured error context including failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternative approaches
options.explanation: Structured error context gives the coordinator all information needed for intelligent recovery â€” retry with modified query, try alternatives, or proceed with partial results.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement automatic retry with exponential backoff, returning generic "search unavailable" after all retries
options.explanation: Generic status hides valuable context from the coordinator, preventing informed recovery decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: Catch the timeout and return an empty result set marked as successful
options.explanation: Error suppression anti-pattern. The coordinator thinks the search found nothing when actually it failed. Prevents recovery; produces incomplete research unknowingly.

options.label: D
options.text: Propagate the timeout exception directly to terminate the entire workflow
options.explanation: Over-reaction. The coordinator could retry, delegate to another subagent, or proceed with partial results. Complete termination wastes all completed work.

questions.question: The synthesis agent frequently needs to verify claims, requiring 2â€“3 round trips through the coordinator to the web search agent (40% latency increase). 85% are simple fact-checks, 15% need deep investigation. What's the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Give the synthesis agent a scoped `verify_fact` tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue through the coordinator
options.explanation: Applies principle of least privilege: gives synthesis only what it needs for the 85% common case while preserving the coordination pattern for complex cases.

options.label: B
options.text: Have synthesis accumulate all verification needs and batch them to the coordinator at the end
options.explanation: Batching creates blocking dependencies â€” synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts. Can't synthesize paragraph 3 if paragraph 1's fact-check hasn't returned.

options.label: C
options.text: Give synthesis access to all web search tools for any verification directly
options.explanation: Over-provisions the synthesis agent, violating separation of concerns and least privilege. Now it's also a search agent. Potentially >5 tools anti-pattern.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the web search agent proactively cache extra context anticipating verification needs
options.explanation: Speculative caching cannot reliably predict what synthesis will need to verify. Over-fetches in most cases while still missing some verification needs.

questions.question: Your pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input. What's the correct approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Add the `-p` flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
options.explanation: The `-p` (or `--print`) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, outputs to stdout, and exits.

options.label: B
options.text: Set the environment variable `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` before running the command
options.explanation: `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` is a fabricated environment variable that does not exist in Claude Code. Trap answer.

options.label: C
options.text: Redirect stdin from `/dev/null`: `claude "..." < /dev/null`
options.explanation: A Unix workaround that doesn't properly address Claude Code's command syntax. Not the designed non-interactive mode.

options.label: D
options.text: Add the `--batch` flag: `claude --batch "..."`
options.explanation: `--batch` is a fabricated flag that does not exist in Claude Code. The real flag is `-p` / `--print`.

questions.question: Your team has two workflows: (1) blocking pre-merge check and (2) overnight technical debt report. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this?

options.label: A
options.text: Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks
options.explanation: Message Batches API has up to 24-hour processing with no latency SLA. Unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks but ideal for overnight batch jobs. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

options.label: B
options.text: Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling
options.explanation: Polling doesn't solve the fundamental problem: batch processing has no guaranteed completion time. Developers could be blocked for hours.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues
options.explanation: Wastes the 50% savings opportunity on overnight reports with zero latency requirements. Batch results can be correlated using `custom_id` fields.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch both to batch with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long
options.explanation: Over-engineered. The simpler solution is matching each API to its appropriate use case. No fallback logic needed.

questions.question: A PR modifying 14 files produces inconsistent review results: detailed feedback for some files, superficial for others, contradictory findings flagging a pattern as bad in one file but approving identical code elsewhere. How should you restructure?

options.label: A
options.text: Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration pass examining cross-file data flow
options.explanation: Splitting reviews into focused passes directly addresses attention dilution. File-by-file analysis ensures consistent depth; a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues.

options.label: B
options.text: Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3â€“4 files
options.explanation: Shifts burden to developers without improving the system. Large PRs are sometimes necessary.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window
options.explanation: Larger context windows don't solve attention quality issues. The problem isn't capacity â€” it's attention distribution.

options.label: D
options.text: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues appearing in at least two
options.explanation: Requiring consensus suppresses real bugs caught intermittently. Reduces sensitivity without addressing root cause.

questions.question:
You are building a customer support agent using the Claude Agent SDK. The agent processes billing disputes by calling tools like `lookup_order`, `get_invoice`, and `process_refund`. A code review notes that after each tool call, your loop checks whether Claude's response text contains the phrase "I have completed" to decide whether to stop.

What is the primary problem with this loop termination approach?

options.label: A
options.text: The agent will never terminate because `process_refund` always returns a success message that prevents "I have completed" from appearing.
options.explanation: Options A and C are based on incorrect assumptions about how tools interact with response text.

options.label: B
options.text: Relying on natural language signals in the assistant's text is unreliable; the correct approach is to inspect `stop_reason` and only terminate when it equals `"end_turn"`.
options.explanation: Inspecting `stop_reason` is the canonical method for agentic loop control: continue iterating when `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"`, stop when it is `"end_turn"`. Natural language phrases like "I have completed" are not reliable termination signals because Claude's phrasing varies across responses and prompt updates.

options.label: C
options.text: The loop should terminate as soon as any tool call fails, since continuing after a failure will corrupt the conversation history.
options.explanation: Options A and C are based on incorrect assumptions about how tools interact with response text.

options.label: D
options.text: Checking response text is only valid in synchronous mode; you must use a callback handler for proper loop control in async contexts.
options.explanation: Option D conflates asynchronous execution patterns with loop control logic.

questions.question:
A developer productivity agent investigates bug reports by calling `search_codebase`, `read_file`, and `run_tests`. During a code review, a colleague proposes adding an iteration cap: if the loop has not finished after 15 tool calls, terminate with a generic "investigation incomplete" response.

What is the correct characterization of this design?

options.label: A
options.text: Iteration caps are the recommended primary stopping mechanism because they prevent runaway costs in production.
options.explanation: Options A and C misstate recommended practice.

options.label: B
options.text: Iteration caps are a reasonable safety boundary for long-running tasks but should not be the primary termination mechanism; `stop_reason: "end_turn"` remains the authoritative signal.
options.explanation: The agentic loop should terminate primarily when `stop_reason` equals `"end_turn"`, which signals that the model has finished its task. An iteration cap can serve as a safety guardrail against runaway loops, but treating it as the primary stopping mechanism means the loop may cut off legitimate multi-step investigations.

options.label: C
options.text: The iteration cap should be replaced with a time-based timeout, since token counts are a more reliable measure of completion than iteration count.
options.explanation: Options A and C misstate recommended practice.

options.label: D
options.text: Iteration caps are unnecessary if the system prompt instructs the agent to always request only the minimum tools needed.
options.explanation: Option D conflates prompt-based guidance with loop control: even a well-prompted agent may legitimately need many tool calls for complex investigations.

questions.question:
Your multi-agent research system's agentic loop appends each tool result to the conversation history before sending the next request. A teammate suggests instead storing all tool results in a separate database and providing only a summary to the model at each iteration, rather than the full result.

Under what condition would this change most likely degrade agent performance?

options.label: A
options.text: When the tool results contain binary data such as images or file attachments.
options.explanation: Option A is a valid concern but not the primary performance risk for text-based research tasks.

options.label: B
options.text: When the model needs to reason across multiple tool results simultaneously to determine its next action, since summaries may omit details required for that reasoning.
options.explanation: The agentic loop depends on tool results being present in conversation history so the model can reason about what it has already discovered before deciding its next action. Summaries may omit field values, error codes, or conditional data the model needs to make correct decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: When the number of tool calls per session exceeds 10, since larger histories slow down the API.
options.explanation: Options C and D are not recognized failure modes for this architecture.

options.label: D
options.text: When tools return results faster than 200ms, making history appending redundant.
options.explanation: Options C and D are not recognized failure modes for this architecture.

questions.question:
Your multi-agent research system uses a coordinator that always routes every query through all four subagents (web search, document analysis, synthesis, and report generation) regardless of query complexity. For a simple factual question like "What year was the Anthropic API released?", the system takes 45 seconds and incurs unnecessary cost.

What design change most directly addresses this?

options.label: A
options.text: Replace the coordinator with a static routing table that maps query keywords to specific subagent pipelines.
options.explanation: Option A replaces adaptive intelligence with a brittle keyword table.

options.label: B
options.text: Have each subagent evaluate its own relevance to the current query and self-select into or out of the pipeline.
options.explanation: Option B distributes coordination logic across subagents, breaking the hub-and-spoke pattern and reducing observability.

options.label: C
options.text: Design the coordinator to dynamically select which subagents to invoke based on query complexity and type, rather than always routing through the full pipeline.
options.explanation: A coordinator that always runs the full pipeline regardless of query complexity wastes resources and adds latency for simple requests. The correct design is a coordinator that evaluates the query and selects only the subagents needed for that specific task.

options.label: D
options.text: Reduce the number of subagents from four to two by merging web search and document analysis into a single "retrieval" agent.
options.explanation: Option D reduces capability unnecessarily instead of improving routing logic.

questions.question:
A coordinator agent in your research system has delegated document analysis to a subagent. After the subagent finishes, the coordinator notices the findings are incomplete: the subagent covered only three of the five specified sources. The coordinator needs to get the remaining two sources analyzed.

What is the correct approach for re-delegating this work?

options.label: A
options.text: Invoke the synthesis agent with the partial findings and instruct it to infer what the missing sources likely contain based on patterns from the three completed analyses.
options.explanation: Option A asks synthesis to fabricate content, which undermines report accuracy.

options.label: B
options.text: The coordinator should re-invoke the document analysis subagent with an explicit prompt specifying only the two missing sources, including the previously completed findings as context.
options.explanation: The coordinator's role includes evaluating output for gaps and re-delegating targeted work to close those gaps. Re-invoking the subagent with only the unfinished sources and providing the completed findings as context is efficient and correct.

options.label: C
options.text: Send all five sources again to a new document analysis subagent instance, which will re-analyze the three already-completed sources along with the two missing ones.
options.explanation: Option C wastes resources by re-processing already-completed sources.

options.label: D
options.text: Let the coordinator generate its own analysis of the remaining two sources directly, rather than spawning another subagent delegation.
options.explanation: Option D bypasses the coordinator-subagent architecture and ignores the subagent's specialization.

questions.question:
You are building a coordinator that delegates research tasks to subagents using the `Task` tool. When you test the system, the coordinator cannot invoke any subagents. Reviewing the coordinator's `AgentDefinition`, you notice the `allowedTools` field is set to `["web_search", "read_document"]`.

What is the most likely cause of the failure?

options.label: A
options.text: The `Task` tool requires an explicit `subagent_endpoint` configuration before it can be invoked.
options.explanation: Option A describes a configuration parameter that does not exist.

options.label: B
options.text: `"Task"` is not included in the coordinator's `allowedTools`, so it cannot spawn subagents.
options.explanation: The `Task` tool is the mechanism for spawning subagents in the Claude Agent SDK. For a coordinator to invoke subagents, `"Task"` must be explicitly included in its `allowedTools`. Simply having other tools available does not grant subagent spawning capability.

options.label: C
options.text: The coordinator's system prompt does not include instructions to use the `Task` tool, so the model never attempts to call it.
options.explanation: Option C conflates prompt instructions with capability gating: even a well-prompted coordinator cannot call a tool that is not in its allowed set.

options.label: D
options.text: Subagent invocation requires the coordinator to be running in plan mode rather than direct execution mode.
options.explanation: Option D conflates plan mode with subagent spawning.

questions.question:
Your multi-agent research system has a web search subagent that consistently returns findings without identifying which sources correspond to which claims. When the synthesis agent receives these findings, it cannot produce properly cited reports.

What is the correct fix during context passing from the web search subagent to the synthesis agent?

options.label: A
options.text: Instruct the synthesis agent to run its own web searches to re-locate the original sources.
options.explanation: Option A introduces redundant work and potentially different search results.

options.label: B
options.text: Use structured data formats that separate claim content from metadata (source URLs, publication dates, page numbers) in the subagent's output, and include this structure when passing context to the synthesis agent.
options.explanation: Structured data formats that separate content from metadata ensure that claim-source mappings survive the handoff between agents. When context is passed as unstructured text, citation information is easily lost.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the coordinator concatenate all subagent outputs into a single text block before forwarding to synthesis, since synthesis will extract citations naturally.
options.explanation: Option C risks losing the claim-source associations during concatenation.

options.label: D
options.text: Configure the web search subagent to return only source URLs, and have the synthesis agent re-read each source to reconstruct the findings.
options.explanation: Option D is extremely inefficient and requires the synthesis agent to redo retrieval work.

questions.question: A coordinator needs to research three independent subtopics in parallel: market trends, competitor analysis, and regulatory environment. Each requires a separate web search subagent. How should the coordinator spawn these subagents to maximize throughput?

options.label: A
options.text: Spawn the subtopics sequentially: start the first subagent, wait for its result, then start the second, and so on, to avoid context conflicts.
options.explanation: Option A introduces unnecessary serial latency.

options.label: B
options.text: Emit all three `Task` tool calls in a single coordinator response, which allows the subagents to run in parallel.
options.explanation: The Claude Agent SDK supports parallel subagent execution by emitting multiple `Task` tool calls in a single coordinator response. This maximizes throughput for independent subtopics that do not depend on each other's results.

options.label: C
options.text: Route all three subtopics through a single subagent sequentially, sharing context between them to reduce total memory usage.
options.explanation: Option C assumes a dependency that the question does not establish.

options.label: D
options.text: Use a single subagent with three separate prompts in sequence, passing prior results as context for each subsequent prompt.
options.explanation: Option D collapses three specialists into one sequential process, eliminating parallelism.

questions.question:
Your customer support agent handles account closure requests. The workflow requires: (1) verify customer identity via `get_customer`, (2) check for active subscriptions via `check_subscriptions`, (3) process closure via `close_account`. Production logs show the agent occasionally calls `close_account` before completing the identity verification step, resulting in unauthorized account closures.

A colleague suggests adding a system prompt instruction: "Always verify identity before closing accounts." What is the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: The system prompt instruction is sufficient because it explicitly describes the required order.
options.explanation: Option A relies on probabilistic compliance, which the logs already show is insufficient.

options.label: B
options.text: Add few-shot examples showing the correct three-step sequence alongside the system prompt instruction.
options.explanation: Option B improves the odds but still does not guarantee compliance.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a programmatic prerequisite that blocks `close_account` from executing until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID.
options.explanation: When a tool ordering requirement has serious consequences (unauthorized account closures), programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees. Prompt instructions have a non-zero failure rate even when explicit.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a routing classifier that analyzes the request type and pre-selects the appropriate tools before the agent begins.
options.explanation: Option D addresses tool selection rather than ordering enforcement.

questions.question:
A customer contacts your support agent about three issues in a single message: a billing charge dispute, a missing order, and a request to update their email address. The agent processes these sequentially, taking 3-4 minutes per request. A senior architect suggests you redesign the handling approach.

What design change would most improve efficiency while maintaining accuracy?

options.label: A
options.text: Instruct the agent to address only the highest-priority issue per conversation turn and ask the customer to submit separate tickets for the remaining issues.
options.explanation: Option A creates a poor customer experience and extra work for the customer.

options.label: B
options.text: Decompose the three concerns into distinct investigation items and process each in parallel using shared customer context, then compile a unified response.
options.explanation: When a customer presents multiple independent concerns, decomposing them into parallel investigation items dramatically reduces latency. Each concern can be investigated simultaneously using the shared customer context already retrieved.

options.label: C
options.text: Process the three issues sequentially but cache intermediate results so subsequent issues benefit from data already retrieved.
options.explanation: Option C improves caching but does not eliminate the serial bottleneck.

options.label: D
options.text: Delegate all three issues to a single specialized "multi-issue" subagent that handles complex requests with multiple concerns.
options.explanation: Option D creates an undifferentiated subagent that doesn't reflect the coordinator-subagent specialization pattern.

questions.question:
Your customer support agent integrates with three backend MCP tools: a legacy billing system returning Unix timestamps, an order management system returning ISO 8601 dates, and a subscription service returning numeric status codes (1=active, 2=paused, 3=cancelled). The agent frequently misinterprets these heterogeneous formats when reasoning about customer records.

What is the most appropriate architectural fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Update each backend system to return a uniform date and status format before the agent calls them.
options.explanation: Option A requires backend changes that may not be feasible and introduces coupling.

options.label: B
options.text: Add format conversion instructions to the system prompt explaining how to interpret each tool's output conventions.
options.explanation: Option B relies on probabilistic compliance and adds token overhead.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement `PostToolUse` hooks that intercept tool results from each source and normalize timestamps, dates, and status codes into a consistent format before the model processes them.
options.explanation: `PostToolUse` hooks are the correct mechanism for intercepting and transforming tool results before the model processes them. This ensures the model always sees normalized data regardless of backend heterogeneity.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-processing step after the agent produces its final response to re-format any dates and statuses that appear in the output.
options.explanation: Option D applies normalization after the model has already reasoned on inconsistent data, which cannot repair any incorrect conclusions already made.

questions.question:
Your customer support agent has a policy: refunds above $500 require manager approval and must not be processed autonomously. Your system prompt states "Do not process refunds above $500 without manager approval." Production logs show this rule is violated in approximately 3% of cases.

What is the most effective way to guarantee compliance?

options.label: A
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt language: "You are strictly forbidden from processing refunds above $500 without explicit manager approval under any circumstances."
options.explanation: Options A and B both improve adherence probabilistically but cannot guarantee zero violations.

options.label: B
options.text: Add 10 few-shot examples in the system prompt, all demonstrating the agent requesting manager approval for high-value refunds.
options.explanation: Options A and B both improve adherence probabilistically but cannot guarantee zero violations.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a hook that intercepts outgoing `process_refund` tool calls, checks the refund amount, and blocks execution or redirects to the manager approval workflow when the amount exceeds $500.
options.explanation: Business rules that require guaranteed compliance must be enforced programmatically, not through prompt instructions alone. A hook that intercepts `process_refund` calls before execution provides deterministic enforcement regardless of how the model interprets the system prompt.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement a validation step that runs after `process_refund` completes and reverses any refunds that exceeded the threshold.
options.explanation: Option D runs after the action has already been taken, which means the policy violation has already occurred.

questions.question:
A developer productivity agent has a `PostToolUse` hook that transforms raw Bash output. Currently the hook appends a formatted summary but also preserves the full raw output in the result passed to the model. Sessions exploring large codebases are hitting context limits faster than expected.

What change would most effectively reduce unnecessary context consumption from tool results?

options.label: A
options.text: Disable the `PostToolUse` hook entirely and let the model process raw Bash output directly.
options.explanation: Option A removes normalization benefits.

options.label: B
options.text: Modify the hook to return only the formatted summary to the model, trimming the verbose raw output rather than preserving it alongside the summary.
options.explanation: `PostToolUse` hooks can trim verbose tool outputs to only the relevant data before the model processes them. Keeping both raw and summary output doubles the context consumption from each tool call. Modifying the hook to return only the formatted summary addresses the root cause directly.

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the model's `max_tokens` parameter to accommodate the additional context from both raw and formatted output.
options.explanation: Option C increases output capacity but does not address the growing input context.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch from `PostToolUse` hooks to pre-processing the Bash commands themselves to produce shorter output.
options.explanation: Option D would require significant changes to tool invocation patterns and may not be feasible for all commands.

questions.question:
You are building an automated code review system using Claude. The review must cover three aspects for every pull request: security vulnerabilities, style compliance, and performance implications. Each aspect has clear, defined criteria documented in your engineering handbook.

Which decomposition strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Dynamic adaptive decomposition: have the agent start by scanning the full PR and generate a review plan based on what it finds.
options.explanation: Option A uses dynamic decomposition for a workflow whose structure is already known, adding unnecessary overhead.

options.label: B
options.text: Prompt chaining with sequential focused passes: one pass per review aspect (security, style, performance), each with dedicated criteria.
options.explanation: When a workflow has predictable, well-defined aspects that must each be covered, prompt chaining with sequential focused passes is the appropriate pattern. Each pass dedicates full attention to one concern rather than dividing attention across all three.

options.label: C
options.text: A single comprehensive pass that examines all three aspects simultaneously to capture cross-cutting concerns.
options.explanation: Option C suffers from the attention dilution problem that prompt chaining is designed to solve.

options.label: D
options.text: Spawn three fully independent agents without shared context, then merge their outputs in a final aggregation step.
options.explanation: Option D loses cross-file integration context by fully isolating the three agents.

questions.question:
An engineering team asks you to design a Claude-based system to investigate a production incident. The system must root-cause a bug that was introduced somewhere in the past two weeks across an unknown set of files and services. The scope of the investigation cannot be known in advance.

Which decomposition approach is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt chaining: design a fixed sequence of investigation steps (check logs, read configs, trace service calls) that the agent executes in order.
options.explanation: Option A is appropriate for predictable multi-aspect reviews, not exploratory investigations.

options.label: B
options.text: Dynamic adaptive decomposition: the agent first maps the affected services and symptom timeline, then generates and prioritizes investigation subtasks based on what is discovered at each step.
options.explanation: Open-ended investigation tasks with unknown scope require dynamic adaptive decomposition, where the agent builds its investigation plan based on intermediate findings rather than following a fixed sequence. A static prompt chain cannot adapt when the investigation reveals unexpected service dependencies or anomalous patterns.

options.label: C
options.text: Split the investigation between two agents: one agent reads logs while the other reads source code, and they report findings independently to a human operator.
options.explanation: Option C removes the coordinating intelligence and requires human intervention for synthesis.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the agent run a comprehensive search of all changed files in the past two weeks and produce a ranked list of candidates for manual review.
options.explanation: Option D is a single-pass heuristic that does not apply the model's reasoning capability.

questions.question:
A developer has been using Claude Code to investigate a legacy authentication service. After several hours, they have built up a detailed session with findings about the service's token validation logic. They need to step away and return tomorrow. Three core files in the authentication module will be modified overnight by another team.

What is the most reliable approach for resuming the investigation productively the next day?

options.label: A
options.text: Resume the named session with `--resume <session-name>` and notify the agent about the specific files that were changed, so it can re-analyze those files in the context of its prior understanding.
options.explanation: When resuming a session after code modifications, the correct approach is to use `--resume <session-name>` to continue the named session and explicitly inform the agent about which files changed so it can target re-analysis appropriately.

options.label: B
options.text: Start a completely new session each time, since stale tool results from the previous session make resumption unreliable for any scenario.
options.explanation: Option B is overly conservative: starting fresh is appropriate when prior tool results are broadly stale, but when only a few known files changed, resumption with targeted context about those changes is more efficient.

options.label: C
options.text: Resume the named session without any notification about file changes; the agent will detect modifications automatically when it next reads those files.
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because the agent does not automatically detect file changes on resume.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `fork_session` to create a branch of the current session before stepping away, then resume from the fork the next day.
options.explanation: Option D misuses `fork_session`, which is designed for exploring divergent approaches from a shared baseline, not for session continuity across time gaps.

questions.question:
You are leading a research investigation using Claude Code. You have completed an initial analysis of a competitor's public API documentation and want to explore two divergent architectural approaches for your response strategy: one focused on feature parity, one focused on differentiation. You want both explorations to start from the same analysis baseline.

Which session management approach best fits this scenario?

options.label: A
options.text: Run two separate new sessions, each with a copy of the analysis findings injected as context in the initial prompt.
options.explanation: Option A requires duplicating context and does not preserve the live session state.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `--resume` to resume the current session twice in parallel, once for each exploration direction.
options.explanation: Option B describes a mechanism that does not work this way: `--resume` continues a single session sequentially, not in parallel branches.

options.label: C
options.text: Use `fork_session` to create two independent branches from the current session, then explore each approach in its respective branch.
options.explanation: `fork_session` is designed precisely for this scenario: creating independent branches from a shared analysis baseline to explore divergent approaches. Each branch inherits the common findings and can be explored independently without contaminating the other.

options.label: D
options.text: Continue in the same session, exploring one approach, then using `/compact` to clear context before exploring the second approach.
options.explanation: Option D uses `/compact` destructively, losing the baseline context needed for the second exploration.

questions.question:
Your customer support agent has a `lookup_order` tool that is called at the right times, but frequently returns errors. Logs show the agent passes free-text descriptions like "order from last Tuesday" instead of the required ISO-8601 timestamp format. The tool description reads: "Retrieves order details given a date or identifier." No examples or format constraints are provided.

What change to the tool definition would most directly reduce these input format errors?

options.label: A
options.text: Add input format constraints, accepted value formats, and example inputs to the tool description so the model knows exactly how to format its calls.
options.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism the model uses to understand how to call a tool. Adding format constraints and examples directly to the description gives the model the information it needs at decision time.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a system prompt instruction: "Always use ISO-8601 format when calling lookup_order."
options.explanation: Adding format instructions to the system prompt adds token overhead but is more fragile than embedding constraints directly in the tool-specific description where the model looks for calling conventions.

options.label: C
options.text: Add input schema validation that rejects malformed inputs and returns an error to the agent.
options.explanation: Validation catches errors after they occur but does not prevent the model from sending malformed inputs in the first place, creating unnecessary error cycles.

options.label: D
options.text: Split `lookup_order` into two tools: `lookup_order_by_id` and `lookup_order_by_date` to reduce ambiguity.
options.explanation: Splitting the tool addresses disambiguation between lookup types rather than the format guidance issue that is causing the errors.

questions.question:
Your customer support agent has three tools with overlapping names and descriptions: `get_account_info` ("Get information about the account"), `fetch_account_details` ("Fetch account details"), and `retrieve_customer_record` ("Retrieve customer information"). All three call different backend systems with different data. The agent frequently picks the wrong tool.

Which approach most effectively resolves the misrouting?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a system prompt instruction listing all three tools and specifying exactly when each should be used based on the request type.
options.explanation: System prompt instructions may reduce misrouting but do not fix the root problem: the tool descriptions themselves are indistinguishable.

options.label: B
options.text: Rename the tools to reflect their distinct data sources and rewrite their descriptions to explain what data each returns, what backend it queries, and what use case it serves.
options.explanation: Ambiguous or overlapping tool names and descriptions cause misrouting. Renaming tools to reflect their distinct backends and writing descriptions that explain what each one returns, where its data comes from, and when to use it are the right fixes.

options.label: C
options.text: Consolidate all three tools into one tool with a `source` parameter that specifies which backend to query.
options.explanation: Consolidating into one generic tool with a parameter shifts the burden of correct routing back to the model in a less structured way.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the current tools but randomize which one the agent calls, then merge the responses in a post-processing step.
options.explanation: Randomizing tool selection and merging responses is not a viable architecture for resolving misrouting.

questions.question:
Your customer support MCP tool `process_refund` returns the same error response for all failures: `{"status": "error", "message": "Operation failed"}`. The agent currently handles all errors by apologizing to the customer and ending the conversation. Logs show this response is triggered by: network timeouts, invalid refund amounts, refunds blocked by fraud detection, and expired order IDs.

What structured error response design would most improve the agent's ability to recover appropriately?

options.label: A
options.text: Return different HTTP status codes for each failure type and have the agent interpret the status code to determine recovery action.
options.explanation: HTTP status codes may not map cleanly to all failure categories and are not as expressive as structured fields for guiding agent recovery.

options.label: B
options.text: Return structured error metadata including `errorCategory` (transient/validation/permission/business), `isRetryable` boolean, and a human-readable explanation specific to the failure reason.
options.explanation: Structured error metadata gives the agent the information it needs to choose the correct recovery path: retry a transient failure, ask the customer for corrected input on a validation failure, or explain a policy block on a business rule violation.

options.label: C
options.text: Return a verbose error log with the full stack trace and system state so the agent has maximum information to reason from.
options.explanation: A full stack trace provides excessive detail that consumes context without improving decision quality for agent recovery.

options.label: D
options.text: Return a numeric error code and have the system prompt map each code to a recovery action.
options.explanation: Encoding recovery logic in the system prompt rather than in a principled error structure makes it harder to maintain as new error types are added.

questions.question:
A web search subagent in your research system calls `search_web` and receives the following response: `{"results": [], "status": "success"}`. The subagent reports to the coordinator: "Web search was successful but no relevant results were found." Later you discover the search API was actually down and returning empty results for all queries.

What change to the MCP tool's error handling would prevent this confusion?

options.label: A
options.text: Always return a non-empty results array by including fallback content when the actual search returns nothing.
options.explanation: Including fallback content masks failures by fabricating results, which is worse than the original confusion.

options.label: B
options.text: Distinguish between access failures (where the backend could not be reached or returned an error) and valid empty results (where the search succeeded but found no matches), using the `isError` flag for the former.
options.explanation: Access failures and valid empty results are fundamentally different conditions that require different agent responses. Using the `isError` flag for access failures while returning empty results only for genuine queries gives the coordinator accurate information to make retry and recovery decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a `confidence` field to the response so the agent can infer whether the empty result is a real outcome or a failure.
options.explanation: A confidence score on a genuinely empty result is meaningless and introduces ambiguity rather than resolving it.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement automatic retry in the MCP tool itself, so the agent never sees an empty result unless all retries were exhausted.
options.explanation: Retries handle transient failures locally but do not solve the agent's inability to distinguish between access failures and valid empty results.

questions.question:
Your MCP tool `check_fraud_risk` returns `{"isError": true, "message": "Refund blocked"}` when a refund is flagged by fraud detection. The agent interprets this as a transient failure and retries the refund three times before giving up. Each retry generates a separate fraud alert in your compliance system.

What is the missing element in the error response design?

options.label: A
options.text: The response should include a `retry_after` timestamp so the agent knows when to retry.
options.explanation: A retry_after timestamp implies the block is temporary and retriable, which is the opposite of the intended behavior for a fraud detection block.

options.label: B
options.text: The response should include `isRetryable: false` and a customer-appropriate explanation distinguishing this business rule block from a transient system error.
options.explanation: A business rule block from fraud detection is a non-retryable error. Including `isRetryable: false` signals to the agent that retrying will not resolve the situation, preventing redundant attempts that trigger compliance alerts. A customer-appropriate explanation helps the agent communicate the block appropriately.

options.label: C
options.text: The tool should suppress the `isError` flag for fraud blocks and instead return the result as a successful response with a `blocked: true` field.
options.explanation: Hiding the error nature of the response makes recovery logic less clear and conflates success with blocked outcomes.

options.label: D
options.text: The response should include the fraud risk score so the agent can decide whether the score is high enough to justify blocking.
options.explanation: Exposing fraud risk data to the agent provides information it may not be authorized to act on directly.

questions.question:
You are building a multi-agent research system with a synthesis agent whose sole job is combining findings from subagents and producing a structured report. You have given the synthesis agent access to all 18 tools in your system: web search, file reading, database queries, email sending, calendar access, and more, reasoning that more tools provide more flexibility.

What problem does this configuration most likely introduce?

options.label: A
options.text: The synthesis agent will refuse to call any tools because it is overwhelmed by the number of choices.
options.explanation: The agent will still call tools, but may call the wrong ones. Refusal is not the typical failure mode for too many tools.

options.label: B
options.text: Having access to tools outside its specialization increases the likelihood the synthesis agent will misuse them, such as initiating new web searches instead of synthesizing the provided findings.
options.explanation: Giving an agent access to tools outside its specialization degrades tool selection reliability. A synthesis agent with web search tools will sometimes initiate new searches rather than working with the findings already provided, breaking the intended workflow.

options.label: C
options.text: 18 tools will exceed the context window limit for tool schemas, causing API errors on every request.
options.explanation: 18 tools with typical schema sizes do not exceed context window limits. This is not a realistic failure mode.

options.label: D
options.text: The additional tools will slow down the synthesis agent because the model must read all tool descriptions before producing output.
options.explanation: This mischaracterizes how tool descriptions affect latency. The primary risk is misuse, not processing speed.

questions.question:
Your customer support system requires that every response from the `draft_response` agent includes a structured JSON summary before the agent returns its output. You want to guarantee the agent calls the `generate_summary` tool on every invocation, not optionally.

Which `tool_choice` configuration achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Set `tool_choice: "auto"` so the model decides when the summary tool is needed.
options.explanation: `auto` allows the model to return text without calling any tool, which does not guarantee the summary is generated.

options.label: B
options.text: Set `tool_choice: "any"` so the model must call at least one tool, though it may choose a different tool instead.
options.explanation: `any` guarantees a tool call but does not guarantee which tool, so the model might choose a different one.

options.label: C
options.text: Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "generate_summary"}` to force the model to call `generate_summary` specifically.
options.explanation: Forced tool selection via `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "generate_summary"}` guarantees the model calls that specific tool on every invocation.

options.label: D
options.text: Remove all other tools from the agent's tool list so `generate_summary` is the only option available.
options.explanation: This achieves the result indirectly but removes legitimate tools the agent may need for its other tasks.

questions.question:
Your team is setting up a shared GitHub MCP server for all engineers on a project. The server requires a GitHub API token for authentication. You want every engineer who clones the repository to have the MCP server available without each person having to manually configure it, and you want to avoid committing the actual token to version control.

What is the correct configuration approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Add the MCP server to `~/.claude.json` on each developer's machine with their personal token hardcoded.
options.explanation: This requires manual per-person setup and hardcodes tokens, which is neither scalable nor secure.

options.label: B
options.text: Add the MCP server to the project-scoped `.mcp.json` file with the token specified using environment variable expansion (e.g., `${GITHUB_TOKEN}`), and commit `.mcp.json` to the repository.
options.explanation: Project-scoped `.mcp.json` with environment variable expansion is the correct pattern for shared MCP servers: it is committed to the repository so all team members get the configuration automatically, and tokens are injected via environment variables at runtime rather than committed as plaintext.

options.label: C
options.text: Add the MCP server configuration to the root `CLAUDE.md` file under a `[mcp_servers]` section.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md does not support MCP server configuration sections. This mechanism does not exist.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a setup script that each developer runs once to add the MCP server to their personal `~/.claude.json` with their token.
options.explanation: This requires manual setup per developer and does not solve the version-control sharing problem.

questions.question:
A developer productivity agent frequently makes multiple exploratory tool calls to discover what data sources are available in your MCP server before it can answer user questions. This pattern increases latency and consumes context for routine requests.

What MCP feature most directly addresses this discovery overhead?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a `list_tools` meta-tool to the MCP server that returns all available tool names and descriptions.
options.explanation: A meta-tool still requires a tool call to discover available resources, adding rather than reducing overhead.

options.label: B
options.text: Expose content catalogs as MCP resources, giving the agent visibility into available data at connection time rather than through exploratory tool calls.
options.explanation: MCP resources are designed to expose content catalogs so agents know what data is available without making exploratory tool calls. This gives the agent upfront visibility at connection time, reducing the need for repeated discovery queries.

options.label: C
options.text: Reduce the number of tools in the MCP server by merging similar tools together to minimize the discovery surface.
options.explanation: Merging tools reduces functionality to avoid a structural problem rather than solving the discovery overhead directly.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a caching layer that stores the results of previous exploratory calls and reuses them across sessions.
options.explanation: Caching addresses the symptom rather than the cause, and cross-session caching of content catalogs may return stale data.

questions.question:
A developer productivity agent needs to find all TypeScript files in a project that import from a deprecated module named `legacy-auth`. The project has thousands of files across many directories.

Which combination of built-in tools is most appropriate for this task?

options.label: A
options.text: Use `Bash` to run `find . -name "*.ts"` and then `Bash` again to run `grep` on each file found.
options.explanation: Using Bash shell commands should be replaced by dedicated tools when those tools are available.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `Glob` to find all `.ts` files, then use `Read` to open each file and check whether it contains the import.
options.explanation: Using Glob for enumeration and then Read on each file is far less efficient than Grep for a content search.

options.label: C
options.text: Use `Grep` to search for the import pattern across all TypeScript files in the codebase.
options.explanation: `Grep` is the correct built-in tool for searching file contents across a codebase. It efficiently searches all TypeScript files for the import pattern without requiring separate file enumeration.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `Read` on the project root directory to get a file listing, then recursively `Read` each subdirectory.
options.explanation: Using Read for directory traversal is not a supported use of that tool.

questions.question:
A developer productivity agent needs to update a configuration value in a file. The value appears in a block of code that contains several nearly identical lines. When the agent uses `Edit`, the tool returns an error: "Match not unique: found 3 occurrences of the target text."

What is the correct fallback approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Use `Bash` to run a `sed` command to replace all occurrences of the target text simultaneously.
options.explanation: Using Bash shell commands when built-in tools should be preferred is not the correct approach, and sed would replace all occurrences rather than targeting the correct one.

options.label: B
options.text: Use `Read` to load the full file contents, identify the exact surrounding context needed to make the edit unique, and retry `Edit` with a larger `old_string` that uniquely identifies the correct location.
options.explanation: When Edit fails due to non-unique text, the correct first step is to use Read to examine the full file and find enough surrounding context to construct a unique `old_string`. This is more surgical than a full Write overwrite.

options.label: C
options.text: Use `Write` to overwrite the entire file with a corrected version, based on the contents loaded by `Read`.
options.explanation: Read + Write is a valid fallback but should only be used when Edit still cannot find a unique anchor even with additional context.

options.label: D
options.text: Use `Grep` to locate the line number of each occurrence, then use `Edit` with a line number parameter to target the correct one.
options.explanation: Edit does not support a line number parameter, so this approach is not possible.

questions.question: A senior engineer adds detailed coding standards and security guidelines to their `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` file. When a new team member joins and clones the repository, they report that Claude Code behaves differently and does not appear to follow the team's documented standards. What is the most likely cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The new team member's Claude Code version is outdated and does not support shared configuration files.
options.explanation: Claude Code version does not affect whether user-scoped files are shared. This describes a mechanism that does not exist.

options.label: B
options.text: The `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` file is user-scoped and not version-controlled, so teammates do not receive it when they clone the repository.
options.explanation: User-level configuration in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` applies only to the individual developer and is never committed to version control. Teammates will not see it regardless of their setup. To share standards across the team, those instructions must live in the project-level `CLAUDE.md` or `.claude/CLAUDE.md`, which are committed to the repository.

options.label: C
options.text: CLAUDE.md files must be placed in the `.claude/` subdirectory to be recognized; a root-level `CLAUDE.md` is ignored.
options.explanation: A root-level CLAUDE.md is a valid project-level location. This statement is incorrect.

options.label: D
options.text: The configuration hierarchy requires the project-level file to explicitly import from user-level files using `@import`.
options.explanation: There is no @import mechanism from user-level to project-level files. This describes a mechanism that does not exist.

questions.question: Your monorepo has a root `CLAUDE.md` that has grown to over 400 lines, covering Python conventions, TypeScript conventions, infrastructure rules, and testing standards. Developers report that Claude sometimes applies the wrong conventions to the wrong files, and the file is difficult to maintain. What is the best approach to reorganize this configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: Split the content into multiple files in `.claude/rules/`, with each file covering a focused topic, and use path-scoped YAML frontmatter to activate rules only for relevant files.
options.explanation: The `.claude/rules/` directory is designed for exactly this scenario: organizing topic-specific rule files with YAML frontmatter path scoping so each rule set activates only when editing relevant files. This reduces irrelevant context and token usage.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a separate `CLAUDE.md` in each top-level package directory and delete the root file entirely.
options.explanation: Creating separate CLAUDE.md files per package requires duplicating shared rules across directories.

options.label: C
options.text: Add inline section headers to the monolithic file and use the `/memory` command to tell Claude which section to prioritize for each task.
options.explanation: Relying on manual intervention each session via /memory is not maintainable for a team environment.

options.label: D
options.text: Break the root `CLAUDE.md` into topic files and use `@import` directives in the root file to pull them all in unconditionally.
options.explanation: Unconditional @import addresses the maintenance concern but not the wrong-conventions problem, since all rules would still load regardless of context.

questions.question: A developer wants a `/scaffold` skill that generates boilerplate for a new microservice. The skill runs many exploratory file reads and Bash commands to understand existing patterns before generating output, producing hundreds of lines of intermediate output. Teammates complain this pollutes their main conversation context. Which frontmatter setting resolves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Set `allowed-tools: []` in the skill's frontmatter to prevent tool use during execution.
options.explanation: Disabling tool use would prevent the skill from performing the exploratory reads and commands it depends on.

options.label: B
options.text: Set `context: fork` in the skill's frontmatter to run the skill in an isolated sub-agent context that does not affect the main session.
options.explanation: The `context: fork` frontmatter option runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context. All exploratory output, intermediate tool calls, and reasoning happen in the fork and do not accumulate in the main conversation context. Only the final output is returned to the parent session.

options.label: C
options.text: Move the skill file from `.claude/skills/` to `.claude/commands/` so it runs as a command rather than a skill.
options.explanation: Moving between skills and commands does not change execution isolation behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: Add `argument-hint: "service-name"` to the frontmatter so the skill receives a clean input without inheriting session context.
options.explanation: argument-hint is for prompting the user for input parameters, unrelated to output isolation.

questions.question: Your team uses Terraform for infrastructure but only in the `infra/` directory. You want Claude to automatically apply Terraform naming conventions and module structure rules only when editing `.tf` files, without those rules appearing in unrelated Python or TypeScript sessions. What is the correct approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Create a `CLAUDE.md` file inside `infra/` that contains the Terraform rules so they apply only within that subdirectory.
options.explanation: A CLAUDE.md inside infra/ would work for files in that directory but cannot use glob patterns to further filter by file type.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a rules file in `.claude/rules/terraform.md` with YAML frontmatter specifying `paths: ["infra/**/*"]` and listing the Terraform conventions.
options.explanation: The `.claude/rules/` directory with YAML frontmatter path scoping is the correct mechanism for conditionally loading conventions. Setting `paths: ["infra/**/*"]` ensures the Terraform rules load only when editing files in that directory tree.

options.label: C
options.text: Add the Terraform rules to the root `CLAUDE.md` under a clearly marked section, and instruct Claude in the system prompt to apply them only to `.tf` files.
options.explanation: Relying on Claude's inference rather than explicit path matching leads to inconsistent behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a skill in `.claude/skills/terraform.md` with `allowed-tools` restricted to file operations on the `infra/` directory.
options.explanation: Skills require manual invocation and do not activate automatically based on file path.

questions.question: A developer asks Claude Code to add a single null-check to one function in a utility module. The function signature, expected behavior, and fix are all clear. Should they use plan mode or direct execution?

options.label: A
options.text: Plan mode, because any code change could have unexpected side effects that need exploration before committing.
options.explanation: This describes an over-cautious heuristic that does not reflect the intended use of plan mode for simple, well-understood changes.

options.label: B
options.text: Direct execution, because the task is well-scoped with a clear fix that does not require architectural decisions or multi-file analysis.
options.explanation: Direct execution is appropriate for simple, well-understood, single-location changes with clear scope. Plan mode is designed for tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, architectural decisions, or multi-file modifications. A null-check to one function meets none of the criteria for plan mode.

options.label: C
options.text: Plan mode, because exploring the codebase first prevents Claude from making assumptions about dependencies.
options.explanation: This describes an over-cautious heuristic. A simple null-check does not require dependency exploration.

options.label: D
options.text: Use direct execution first, but run a quick plan mode scan afterward to catch any side effects before committing.
options.explanation: Plan mode is used before execution to explore and decide, not after as a post-execution check. This inverts the correct workflow.

questions.question: A developer is using plan mode to explore a large codebase before deciding how to approach a library migration. During exploration, Claude generates dozens of tool results including full file reads, search outputs, and dependency traces. The developer notices the main conversation context is filling up rapidly and fears context exhaustion before the plan is complete. What is the most appropriate technique to preserve main session context during verbose exploration?

options.label: A
options.text: Switch to direct execution partway through exploration so that Claude uses fewer tool calls.
options.explanation: Switching to direct execution abandons the architectural exploration prematurely.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the Explore subagent to isolate verbose discovery output, having it return a structured summary to the main session rather than accumulating raw results.
options.explanation: The Explore subagent is specifically designed to isolate verbose discovery work from the main conversation context. It performs the exploration and returns a structured summary, preventing raw tool results from consuming the main session's context budget.

options.label: C
options.text: Run `/compact` immediately after each tool call to keep the context window from growing.
options.explanation: Running /compact after every tool call is operationally cumbersome and loses detail that may still be needed.

options.label: D
options.text: Restrict Claude to reading only entry point files during plan mode to limit tool call volume.
options.explanation: Artificially limiting the exploration would produce an incomplete analysis for complex migrations.

questions.question: A developer asks Claude Code to reformat date strings in a data pipeline. After the first attempt, the output format is partially correct but inconsistent: some dates are formatted as `YYYY-MM-DD`, others as `MM/DD/YYYY`. Prose instructions like "always use ISO 8601" have been tried twice without improvement. What technique is most likely to resolve the inconsistency?

options.label: A
options.text: Rewrite the instruction to be more emphatic, using capitalization and repetition to signal importance.
options.explanation: Emphasis does not resolve ambiguity. Capitalization and repetition are unlikely to improve results.

options.label: B
options.text: Provide 2-3 concrete input/output examples showing the exact transformation expected, including edge cases like two-digit years and ambiguous formats.
options.explanation: Concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate expected transformations when prose instructions are interpreted inconsistently. Showing `"3/15/2024" -> "2024-03-15"` is unambiguous in a way that "use ISO 8601" is not.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to plan mode so Claude can explore the codebase before formatting dates.
options.explanation: Plan mode is for architectural decisions, not formatting refinement.

options.label: D
options.text: Ask Claude to generate a formatting function first, then apply it in a second pass.
options.explanation: This adds complexity and still does not clarify what the correct transformation looks like.

questions.question: A development team is using Claude Code to implement a new authentication module. Before any implementation begins, the team lead wants to ensure Claude surfaces potential security considerations, edge cases, and design tradeoffs that the team may not have anticipated. Which iterative refinement technique is most appropriate here?

options.label: A
options.text: Write a detailed specification document and pass it to Claude for direct implementation.
options.explanation: Direct implementation from a specification bypasses the opportunity to surface gaps before implementation is locked in.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the interview pattern: ask Claude to question the team about their requirements, constraints, and assumptions before proposing a design.
options.explanation: The interview pattern is designed to surface design considerations and uncover assumptions the developer may not have anticipated before any implementation begins. It is particularly valuable in domains like security where overlooked edge cases carry high risk.

options.label: C
options.text: Start with a minimal implementation and iterate by describing issues found during code review.
options.explanation: This defers the discovery of design issues until after implementation, when the cost of changes is higher.

options.label: D
options.text: Provide a complete test suite first and ask Claude to write code that passes all tests.
options.explanation: Test-driven iteration is effective for well-defined behavior but cannot surface considerations the team has not yet thought of.

questions.question: Claude Code generated a data transformation function that has three separate issues: an off-by-one error in a loop, a missing null check for an optional field, and an incorrect sort order. Each issue is in a completely different part of the function and none of them interact. What is the recommended approach for addressing these issues?

options.label: A
options.text: Report all three issues in a single detailed message so Claude has full context for all fixes at once.
options.explanation: Sending all issues in a single message is suited to interacting problems, not independent ones.

options.label: B
options.text: Fix them sequentially: address each issue in a separate message and verify the fix before moving to the next.
